title: Session display Phase 3 — Detach (detachable windows)
body:
## Requirement

**Session display Phase 3 — Detach: detachable session windows.** Move a session into its own OS window (draggable to a 2nd monitor), session keeps running; reattach back into the main window.

- **Legacy stays the default** (non-breaking); detach is an **action**, not a third exclusive mode. Controlled via setting `global.sessionDisplayMode`.
- Builds on **Phase 1 tabs (#6)** + **Phase 2 settings window (#7)** (both done) — uses the multi-window pattern proven there.
- Central change: route `terminal-data` per session to the owning window (sidebar/attention events stay in the main window).

Detailed design and implementation plan → comment.

title: Terminal: working-status line renders stacked/duplicated (rare) — distinct from #85 garble
body:
## Requirement

The Claude working-status line occasionally renders **stacked/duplicated** — several
successive status frames sit on top of each other (e.g. three `Hashing…` lines with
*different* content: `22 24`, `16s ↓401`, `11s ↓138 · thought for 5s`) instead of the
single line overwriting in place. A resize heals it. **Occurs rarely.** Seen on build
`main @ 7e473e7` (which already carries the DPR-refit fix), WebGL off (DOM renderer).

This is **distinct from #85** (that was the horizontal char-drift *garble* = xterm.js#6015,
addressed by the DPR-refit). This is **vertical duplication** — old status rows not cleared
when the TUI rewrites the status line — a different mechanism.

**Candidate:** xterm.js#5801 (OPEN) — "ED2 (`\x1b[2J`) inside a DEC 2026 synchronized update
block resets viewportY, causing scroll yank during streaming redraws." Matches stacked status
frames during sync redraws. To be confirmed.

**Expected:** a single, in-place-updating status line; no stacking.

## Diagnosis (in progress)

Temporary probe added on a debug branch (never merged): **F5** = pure `terminal.refresh(0, rows-1)`
(render repaint), **F8** = `safeFit` (geometry re-fit) on the focused session. Press each on the
stacked ghost:
- F5 clears it → dirty-row render-ghost → a refresh-heal fixes it.
- F5 fails, F8/resize clears it → geometry/re-fit class.
- Neither → buffer/sync-viewport (xterm.js#5801) → upstream / deeper.

Keep open until the mechanism is identified.

title: WebGL context loss leaves a stale fit (no re-fit, no repaint)
body:
`WebglAddon.onContextLoss` (`public/terminal-manager.js`) disposes the addon and marks the DOM renderer as suggested, but never re-fits the terminal.

xterm silently continues on its DOM renderer, whose cell metrics differ from WebGL's (xterm.js#6015). Every other renderer switch compensates: `loadTerminalWebgl()` calls `safeFit()` after loading, and `showSession()` re-fits on the next frame when it restores a context suspended by the grid's off-screen handling. The loss path does neither.

Result: a visible terminal that loses its GL context keeps a fit computed for WebGL cell metrics while rendering with DOM metrics — the bottom row can clip, the same failure mode #81 traced to renderer swaps under fractional display scaling. It self-heals only on the next tab switch, when `showSession()` reloads WebGL and re-fits.

Raising the GL-context budget to 32 narrows the window but does not close it: driver-side losses still occur.

### Acceptance
- `onContextLoss` re-fits the terminal and forces a full repaint.
- The re-fit is deferred by one frame — cell metrics are reported only after the renderer swap has painted, matching the existing restore path.
- No re-fit is attempted on a terminal destroyed between the loss and the frame.

Split out of #81 (found while re-reviewing its WebGL invariants).

title: Onboarding: first-time-launch welcome screen
body:
Show a welcome screen on the very first launch to orient a new user, then never again unless reset.

## Requirement

- On first launch (fresh install — no global settings yet), show a welcome overlay: brief intro to the app + primary actions.
- **Actions**: "Import settings" (ties into settings import — migrate from another machine) and "Open settings" (first-time configuration), plus a Dismiss/close.
- Dismissing persists a flag so it never shows again (store `welcomeDismissed` in the global settings blob via `merge-setting`, so it rides existing persistence + export/import).
- Style matches existing dialogs (`new-session-overlay` pattern). UI text English.

Depends on the settings export/import feature for the "Import settings" action.

Plan/design in comments.

title: The project list should be a list — a registry, not a derivation
body:
## The problem

A project that Switchboard knows about, that is not hidden, and that is marked **trusted** can still be impossible to see or to start a session in. `D:/Projekte/example-a` and `D:/Projekte/example-b` are real examples: the Projects admin lists them, badges them `config-only`, shows their cost and token history — and the sidebar shows nothing.

Behind it sit three separate defects with one root: **there is no project list.**

## Why

**1. The sidebar's projects are DERIVED, every time.** They come from the Claude store — session rows plus store folders (`session-cache.js:836-859`) — and a folder is only usable if a project path can be derived from it. `deriveProjectPath()` reads that path out of the `cwd` of a **transcript**:

```js
if (!projectPath) continue;     // session-cache.js:846 — no .jsonl, no project
```

So a project with no transcripts cannot appear, whatever else the app knows about it.

**2. The registry is not a registry — it is a filter.**

```js
// session-cache.js:920-923
if (global.projectAutoAdd === false && Array.isArray(global.addedProjects)) {
  const added = new Set(global.addedProjects);
  return projects.filter(p => added.has(p.projectPath));   // subtractive only
}
```

`addedProjects` is applied **only in manual mode**, and it can only **remove**. So `window.api.addProject(path)` ticks a box on a filter: if the path has no sessions, adding it does nothing at all, however often you do it.

**3. "Remove" is really "hide".** `remove-project` (`main.js:709-730`) pushes the path onto `hiddenProjects`, drops it from the allowlist and purges the cached rows. `hiddenProjects` filters the sidebar **always** — so a removed project stays gone even if ten new sessions appear in it tomorrow. Hiding and deleting are the same operation today, and neither is what the other should be.

## The model

The list becomes a **stored list**. Both modes feed the same list; they differ only in **who may write to it**.

| mode | who registers a project |
|---|---|
| **Add projects automatically** | discovery does — a session found in **any backend's store** (Claude, Codex, Hermes, Pi), including one started outside Switchboard, registers its project. The user may also add one by hand. |
| **Manual** | only the user. |

And two actions that are finally different things:

| action | meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hide** | I do not want to see it — not even when new sessions appear. Reversible from the hidden list. |
| **Remove from Switchboard** | out of the list, cached rows purged. In **auto** mode, fresh activity brings it back; in **manual** mode it stays gone until re-added. Re-adding rescans the folder, so **all** its sessions return — the store files are the truth, the list is only a view of them. |

### Remove needs a memory, or it does nothing

The sessions that put a project on the list **stay on disk** when it is removed. A registry without memory would re-register it on the very next scan, so removing would be a no-op in auto mode — which is exactly why today's code turned "remove" into "hide" instead.

So a removal records **when**: a tombstone `{ path, deletedAt }`. Auto-registration then re-registers a project only on a session **newer than** its tombstone.

| situation | result |
|---|---|
| removed; the old sessions are still on disk | stays gone |
| afterwards a session appears there (in Switchboard or outside it, any backend) | comes back — and the rescan brings **all** its sessions, old ones included |
| re-added by hand | same |
| manual mode | stays gone until the user adds it |

### Cleaning up the tombstones — and the trap in it

A tombstone exists for exactly one purpose: to stop **old** sessions from re-registering the project. Drop it while those sessions still exist and the project **resurrects itself** on the next scan — the cleanup would undo the deletion.

So the load-bearing condition is **not** "the directory is gone from disk". A project directory and its transcript store are different places: the directory can be deleted while `~/.claude/projects/<encoded>/` keeps every transcript. (Observed on a real store: one project had no store folder at all, another had a store folder holding no transcripts.)

```
drop a tombstone when:
      no session remains, in ANY backend store, for that path
  AND it is older than X                          <- the safety belt
```

The age is the belt, not the criterion. It matters because of network drives: an unmounted `Z:\` looks exactly like "the folder is gone", and without a grace period every tombstone on it would be swept away and every project on it would come back the moment it reconnects.

With no sessions left, the tombstone guards nothing: a genuinely new session at that path *should* re-register the project. The list therefore keeps only the entries that still do something, and empties itself as stores are pruned.

**"Delete for good"** needs no second button, then: *Delete this project's sessions* already exists (`deleteProjectSessions`). Removing the transcripts makes the tombstone collectable on the next sweep. Permanence is a consequence, not a mechanism.

## What has to change

- A stored project list (a register), not a derivation. `addedProjects` becomes additive and applies in **both** modes — or moves somewhere better (see the open questions).
- Auto-registration hangs off the **generic** scan, not the Claude one: a Codex/Pi/Hermes session in an unknown path must register its project exactly like a Claude session. Discovery already buckets every backend's rows into projects (`session-cache.js:652-667`); what is missing is the step that writes the entry.
- Registered projects render **without sessions** — an empty, launchable row. The rendering side already exists (`session-cache.js:848-858`); only the source is missing.
- `remove-project` splits into **hide** and **remove** (+ tombstone), and the tombstone sweep.
- Projects admin: a `config-only` row gets an **"add to sidebar"** action, so the badge is an offer instead of a dead end.
- **Migration.** The implicit list today is "everything derived, minus `hiddenProjects`". The register has to be seeded from that on first run, or the sidebar looks different after the update than it did before it.

**Deliberately NOT proposed:** auto-showing every `config-only` project. `~/.claude.json` knows every project Claude has ever touched (this install alone carries 38 on the hidden list) — that would flood the sidebar. Registering stays a decision.

## Open questions

1. Where does the register live — `addedProjects` in the global settings blob (once additive), or its own table? A per-project row already exists (`project_meta`, for favourites and auto-hide), and the tombstones need a home too.
2. How does an empty registered project sort? It has no `lastActivity`.
3. What is X for the tombstone sweep?
4. Does launching a session in an unregistered path auto-register it even in **manual** mode? (Today `ensureProjectAdded` does exactly that — `main.js:3553`.)
5. A project that is BOTH hidden and removed — can that happen, and what wins?

title: Stats: a backend filter shows an empty page as soon as a template runs on that backend
body:
## Problem

Click a backend's pill in Stats and the whole page comes back **empty** — no heatmap, no daily bars, no cost, no models, zero sessions — as soon as **one template** runs on that backend. Reproduced on a real install: the store holds Codex sessions and one Codex template; filtering by Codex reports **0 sessions** while the sidebar plainly shows one.

| filter | sessions reported |
|---|---|
| All | 198 |
| Claude | 186 |
| Hermes | 4 |
| Pi | 5 |
| **Codex** | **0** ← three sessions exist |

Claude only works because no template runs on it (see below).

## Why

A backend filter correctly expands to *the backend plus every template that runs on it* — as an **array** (`main.js:1698-1708`):

```js
function backendFilterIds(backendId) { … return ids; }   // e.g. ['codex', 'test-1']
```

`stats-queries.js` is ready for that: it emits one `?` per id and returns `{sql, params}`.

`db.js:1708-1716` throws all of it away:

```js
function runStats(name, backendId) {
  const only = backendId && backendId !== 'all' ? String(backendId) : null;   // ['codex','test-1'] -> "codex,test-1"
  const cacheKey = name + (only ? ':one' : ':all');                           // one cached stmt regardless of id count
  …
  return only ? stmt.all(only) : stmt.all();                                  // spec.params discarded
}
```

- `String(array)` joins it into a single value no `backendId` will ever equal.
- The prepared statement gets **one** placeholder, whatever the id count.
- The `params` the query module built are never bound.

With no template, `String(['claude'])` happens to be `'claude'` — which is exactly why the bug is invisible until someone creates their first template, and then hits it on the one backend they use most.

## Why no test caught it

`test/stats-queries.test.js` binds `spec.params` **itself** (e.g. `:105`, `:306`). It tests the query module — which is correct — not `db.js`'s plumbing, which is where the defect is.

## Requirement

1. `runStats` must honour an **array**: bind `spec.params`, and key the statement cache on the **id count** (a statement prepared for one placeholder must not be reused for two).
2. A test at the `db.js` level — the real SQL, run through the real `runStats`, with a backend that has a template — so the plumbing is covered and not just the query builder.
3. The twin, renderer-side: the per-backend breakdown filters on raw equality (`id === statsBackendFilter`, `public/stats-view.js:437`), so template sessions vanish from the cards too. It must expand a backend to its templates the way the SQL does.

## Notes

Found while auditing #153 (whose "Stats filter scope" clause reads as done — the *mechanism* is there; this is the wiring under it).

title: An unset $VAR disappears silently at launch — the check exists, it just fires at the wrong moment
body:
## Problem

A template (or launcher) whose env bundle references `$OPENAI_API_KEY` launches happily when that variable is **not set**. The reference is dropped, the key is simply absent, the CLI fails on auth — and **nothing anywhere says why**. The user is left staring at a provider error with no connection to the missing variable.

## Why

`resolveEnv()` drops an unset reference with no return value, no log, no callback (`env-refs.js:44-52`; the drop is even pinned by `test/env-refs.test.js:32-38`). Every spawn path calls it **bare**:

- `main.js:1367` — external launcher
- `main.js:3693` — in-app launcher / terminal tab
- `main.js:3932-3936` — the backend PTY spawn

None of them inspects what was dropped.

**The check already exists.** `env-refs-check` (`main.js:3358-3366`) has three renderer callers, and all of them run in an **editor**:

- `public/backends-panel.js:632` — a live "not set ✗" pill while typing
- `public/launchers-panel.js:135` — the same pill
- `public/backends-panel.js:724-738` — the only actual save-time confirm

So the app can explain the problem precisely — it just does so at the moment nothing is at stake, and stays silent at the moment it costs the user a failed session.

Note the launchers editor has **no** save-time confirm at all, only the inline pill.

## Requirement

Surface unresolved refs **at launch**:

- The spawn path resolves the env bundle; when a referenced variable is unset, say so — naming the variable and the template — instead of starting a session that cannot authenticate.
- Refuse, or warn and continue? Worth deciding: a missing `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` on a template pointed at another provider is fatal, but not every dropped ref is. The honest minimum is that it is **said out loud** and lands in the log.

## Note

A save-time check can never replace a launch-time one: the variable can vanish **between** the save and the launch (a changed host environment, a machine that never had it, a settings file carried across machines). The editor's pill is a nicety; the launch is where it matters.

Split out of #153 (clause: "Unresolved $VAR at launch").

title: Remote repo over SSH (treat like local)
body:
## Requirement

**Remote repo over SSH (treat like local)** _(Source: supacode)_ — open a repo/project on a remote host via SSH and manage it in Switchboard like a local one: session scan, terminal (PTY over SSH), file/diff access. **Large** — needs its own detailed plan (transport, auth via Bitwarden SSH agent, remote `~/.claude/projects` scan, PTY bridging, watcher replacement). Clarify scope/architecture before implementation.

title: Detect subagent spawn via .meta.json (not only .jsonl)
body:
## Requirement

**Detect subagent spawn via `.meta.json` (not only `.jsonl`)** — when spawning a task subagent, Claude Code immediately writes `<parent>/subagents/agent-<id>.meta.json` (`agentType`, `description`, `toolUseId`, `spawnDepth`), but the `.jsonl` transcript only once the agent produces output. A spawned agent that did not (yet) run to producing output (queued, parent turn ends/is interrupted) has **only** `meta.json` → `detectSubagentTransitions`/`enumerateSessionFiles` (which only scan `subagents/*.jsonl`) never see it (observed 2026-07-02: session `876c2b28`, "Feldtexte W6" agents — meta identical to runnable W5 agents, but without jsonl; parent last at 22:26, spawns at 22:28). **No special background-agents format, no data loss — pure visibility.** Fix: also scan `subagents/*.meta.json` → placeholder subagent row (name from `description`, status "spawned/empty/aborted") as soon as the meta is there; upgrade to a full row when the `.jsonl` arrives. Files: `read-session-file.js` (`enumerateSessionFiles`), `session-transitions.js`, `session-cache.js`, subagent rendering in `public/sidebar.js`.

title: Per-session API key override
body:
## Requirement

**Per-session API key override** _(split off from #47)_ — optional password field in the session dialogs that injects `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` **only transiently** per session into the PTY `env` (`open-terminal`, `main.js`) — **never** in the command string or the settings DB. Persistence only via `saved_variables` `secret` (`safeStorage`). Picker layer from `brianstanley` @ `fb81d18`. **Net-new** (we are OAuth/Claude-only) — before implementing, clarify whether it's even wanted.

title: Make the terminal LRU cap configurable
body:
The renderer keeps at most 12 live xterm instances (`TERMINAL_LRU_CAP` in `public/terminal-manager.js`); the least-recently-shown closed session beyond the cap is destroyed (soft cap: running and active sessions are never evicted).

Since WebGL is the default renderer, the cap also serves as the GL-context budget (Chromium allows ~16 per process; a lost context falls back to the DOM renderer).

Expose the cap as a setting for users with many parallel sessions and enough RAM. Constraints: keep 12 as the default; values above ~14 should hint that terminals beyond the GL budget silently fall back to the DOM renderer.

Deferred from #81 (was rejected there as "defer until the cap is hit").

title: Evaluate: multi-sidebar panes
body: Evaluate porting aaaron `feat/multi-sidebar-panes` — multiple sidebar panes. Source: aaaronmiller/switchboard @ feat/multi-sidebar-panes.

title: Evaluate: local Whisper voice input
body: Evaluate porting ivandobsky `feat/local-whisper-voice` — local Whisper voice control/input. Source: ivandobskygithub/switchboard @ feat/local-whisper-voice.

title: Evaluate: brianstanley cursor-ui-refresh
body: Evaluate brianstanley `cursor-ui-refresh` — UI refresh. Source: brianstanley/switchboard @ cursor-ui-refresh. We already ported brianstanley's Variables + preview panel; check what's new/portable.

title: Optional second tray icon showing Claude usage (live percentage)
body:
Add an optional **second system-tray icon** that shows current Claude usage, enabled via Settings (off by default).

## Requirement
- A live-rendered tray icon showing the current usage percentage, styled like a usage monitor: a **colored progress ring** or a **badge** with the number baked into the icon, color-coded by the existing warn/critical thresholds (green → amber → red).
- **Tooltip** with the details we already show in the status bar (5h / 7d, extra usage, reset time).
- Small **context menu**: show/hide the window, open Stats, refresh, quit.
- **Cross-platform**: macOS, Windows, Linux.
- **Reuse the usage data the app already has** — the same values the status bar renders. **No separate polling loop / no extra network fetch** for the tray; it updates whenever the existing status-bar usage updates.
- Enabled + styled via a **Settings** toggle (icon style ring/badge). When off, the second tray is not created; toggling off destroys it.

Reference behaviour: a small pystray + Pillow usage-monitor tray that draws a ring/badge with the percentage and threshold colors.

title: Terminal: evaluate VSCode-style shell integration (OSC 633) for plain terminals
body:
Idea from the VSCode draw-path comparison (#87/#114 context). VSCode's shell integration injects a shell profile hook that emits OSC 633 sequences (command start / command end / exit code / cwd), enabling command tracking, "run recent command", per-command scrollback navigation, and precise prompt detection.

**Requirement:** evaluate whether a slim variant is worth it for our **plain terminals** (not Claude sessions — the CLI is a fullscreen TUI where command tracking is meaningless): e.g. exit-code badges, jump-to-previous-command in scrollback, or more reliable "shell is idle" detection. Likely low value for this app's core use case; document a decision either way. Large feature — only pursue with a concrete user-facing benefit.

title: i18n: renderer localisation support layer (language-neutral, EN master)
body:
Add a language-neutral localisation **support layer** to the renderer, so any user-facing UI string can be translated. This issue is about the **infrastructure**, not the translations themselves — English stays the master/source of truth; concrete languages are added on top afterwards.

## Requirement (the support layer)

- A global `t('area.key', { placeholders })` available to every renderer script — no build step, no module system (matches our vanilla `<script>` setup).
- **English is the master.** Missing keys fall back to English, then to the key itself — the UI is never blank.
- Static HTML localised via `data-i18n` / `data-i18n-title` / `data-i18n-placeholder` / `data-i18n-aria` attributes, filled on load.
- Active language is a global setting (Settings UI). Changing it persists and re-renders every view in the new language.
- Extract the existing English UI strings (sidebar tabs/chrome, session filters, settings labels/dialogs/tooltips) into the dictionary, keyed. No behaviour change while only EN exists.
- Language-neutral by design: adding a language = adding one dictionary, no code changes.

## Rollout (separate follow-up issues, not this one)

Language order once the layer lands: **EN (master) → DE → FR**. Each added language is its own translation task on top of this support layer.

## Work organisation

Large, cross-cutting change (touches nearly every renderer file that emits text) → **own feature branch**.

Reference implementation for the layer: kreaddis `public/i18n.js` (~488 lines) — adapt the mechanism, ignore its specific fr strings. Upstream commit `6e4d127` (fr apostrophe fix) is only relevant once translations exist.

Plan/design + string-extraction strategy go in comments.

title: Multi-LLM: leftover gaps against the plan and the mockups
body:
Small clauses from the multi-LLM plan that were never implemented. Audited against the code on 2026-07-13 (see the comments): of the original seven, **one was done, one is a wontfix, two turned out to be bugs in their own right, and one belongs with the project manager**. What is left:

- **Codex title source**: the plan named `session_index.jsonl` (`thread_name`) as the preferred title source; the parser only uses the first real user prompt (`backends/codex/parser.js:151-155`).

  Bigger than it reads. The side-file is **not documented at all** in `docs/backend-formats.md`, so the work is "record what it actually contains, then use it". And on a real install it is **stale and partial**: 4 entries, last written months ago, against 7 session files — five of them newer. So a per-session **fallback is mandatory**, not polish: the index misses the common case.

- **Launch picker cosmetics**: no "CLI backends" group label, and the default backend's row carries no `default` tag (both in the mockup). Confirmed still true after #163 rebuilt that picker: `public/dialogs.js` holds exactly one `popover-group-label` and it belongs to "Terminal"; `_defaultBackendId` is read **nowhere** in that file, and no other mechanism marks the default (no class, no data attribute, no tooltip — and the sort is Claude-first, not default-first).

- **Cold-scan timing**: the startup scan duration was measured once by hand; it is not instrumented, so a regression would go unnoticed. No `performance.now()` outside `public/terminal-manager.js`, and no elapsed-time log anywhere on the scan path — `session-cache.js:719` logs **counts**, the worker posts a progress **counter**. This one has grown in value: #157 added a path that re-reads the whole store on a parser bump, and nobody would notice it doubling.

---

### Moved out of this list

- **Stats filter scope** — the mechanism landed with #159 (one filter, resolved in SQL, scoping every figure). But the audit found the wiring under it is broken: → **#168** (a backend filter shows an empty page as soon as a template runs on that backend). Struck here so it is not re-done; tracked there because it is a live bug.
- **Unresolved `$VAR` at launch** — not a leftover, a bug: the check exists and fires in the editor, never at the spawn. → **#169**.
- **Projects admin "Backends" column** — moved to **#171**, where it belongs: the project manager is single-backend across the board (trust writes only Claude's config, remap moves only Claude's sessions), and the missing badge is one symptom of that, not a stray cosmetic.
- **Scheduled runs record no backend overlay** — **wontfix**. The scheduler writes its Claude JSONL directly and spawns via `child_process`, so it never reaches the single `sessionBackends.record()` call site (`main.js:3939`). It leaves `backendId` NULL — and every consumer folds NULL → `claude` (`stats-queries.js:24`, `db.js:1284-1295`, `public/backend-registry.js:61`). Stats, badge, search and the scoped deletes all treat it as Claude, which is what it is. The only behavioural gap on that path (the enable-gate) was closed by #162.

title: Session tags: filter the sidebar by session tag
body:
## Problem

Session tags can be defined (Settings → Session tags) and, once #99 lands, assigned to a session via the tag picker. But there is no way to *use* them: nothing filters or searches by session tag, so a tag is a sticker — you can put it on and look at it, and that is all.

Project tags (#98) already have the filter this is missing: a chip bar (`#project-tag-filters`) with AND multi-select, applied in `refreshSidebar()`.

## Requirement

Filter the sidebar by session tag.

- Chip bar of the session tags in use, AND multi-select (same semantics as the project-tag filter).
- Applied in `refreshSidebar()` (app.js): drop non-matching sessions from `p.sessions`, drop projects left empty — the search branch already does exactly this and is the model.
- `hidden` tags keep their chips but stay out of the filter bar; `disabled` tags appear nowhere (#138 semantics).
- Pure matching logic in its own module (mirror `project-tags-filter.js`), with tests.

## Notes

- The sessionId → tags map is already in memory (`tagCache` in `bookmarks-tags.js`, fed by `sessionTagsAll()`). No new IPC, no schema change.
- Open UI question: project and session tags are separate namespaces (`kind` in `tag_defs`). Two chip bars side by side would be noisy — decide between one bar with a Projects/Sessions switch, or showing the session bar only when session tags exist. Best decided with a few tagged sessions on screen.

Depends on #99 (without an entry point there is nothing to filter by).

title: PTY liveness can hold a session busy forever — it has no ceiling
body:
## Problem

For the store-derived backends (Hermes, Pi), busy/idle has a safety net: a turn that has written nothing for longer than the activity window is only kept busy if the terminal is still talking (D21 — `ctx.lastOutputMs`).

That net has **no outer bound**. `session._lastOutputAt` is refreshed on *every* PTY data chunk (`main.js`, the terminal-data handler), and its own comment admits what that includes: "a spinner frame is output, and so is an echoed keystroke". So as long as the TUI produces anything at all once a minute — a cursor blink, a clock, a repaint — the liveness branch returns `busy` indefinitely, however stale the store row is.

```js
// backends/{hermes,pi}/state.js, the tail of deriveState:
const out = Number(opts.lastOutputMs || 0);
return (out && nowMs - out <= OUTPUT_LIVENESS_MS) ? BUSY : IDLE;
```

This is the "permanently working" failure this repo has already shipped twice, reachable by a third door: it needs only a session that is *stuck* in the running-turn branch (a store row that never gets its closing message — a crash, a lost write, a state we misread) plus a TUI that repaints at rest.

## Requirement

Put a ceiling on it. The terminal may keep a turn out of idle for a while; it may not do so forever. Something like: once the store row has been silent for N× the activity window, the session goes idle regardless of what the terminal is doing — the store is the state, output was only ever the tie-breaker.

Whatever N is, it must be long enough for the case the net exists for (a real turn thinking or running a long tool without writing a message) and short enough that a wedged session heals itself within a working session.

## Notes

- Applies to **both** `backends/hermes/state.js` and `backends/pi/state.js` — same shape, same net, same missing ceiling. Fix one, check its sibling (the parity rule).
- Found while verifying #165. Not caused by it: the branch predates that work, which only made it easier to reach.
- `test/backend-parity.test.js` already asserts the liveness rule for both backends; the ceiling belongs there too.
